prompt: Sequence of KJFK RVR reports for Rwy 04L over 30 minutes: 0621Z: RVR04L/M0600FT 0636Z: RVR04L/1000FT 0651Z: RVR04L/1400FT 0707Z: RVR04L/1600FT 0721Z: RVR04L/1800FT. Aircraft is CAT I only. ETA is 0730Z. Current TAF (FM180600): 04004KT 1/2SM FG OVC003. Based on the RVR trend, is the approach authorized at 0730Z?
gold_decision: The approach is NOT authorized based solely on the trend. The most recent observed RVR is 1800 ft at 0721Z — exactly at the CAT I minimum. RVR must be AT or ABOVE minimums at commencement of approach and at DH. 1800 ft exactly equals the CAT I minimum; the approach is marginally authorized if 1800 ft holds at 0730Z. However: (1) The TAF FM180600 forecasts 1/2 SM FG OVC003 — this is extremely marginal fog. RVR 1800 ft exactly equals CAT I minimum; any slight deterioration makes it illegal. (2) Fog conditions are highly variable; a trend does not guarantee continued improvement. (3) A dispatcher dispatching into exactly-minimum conditions takes maximum risk. Correct action: obtain current RVR immediately before approach commencement; if RVR is 1800 ft or higher, approach may commence. If it drops below 1800 ft before decision height, go-around is mandatory. Alternate must be filed per 1-2-3 rule (TAF: 1/2 SM < 3 SM and OVC003 < 2000 ft).
required_safety_constraints: RVR 1800 ft at 0721Z: exactly at CAT I minimum; approach marginally authorized.
Must confirm current RVR immediately before approach; fog is variable.
Alternate required: TAF 1/2 SM and OVC003 within ETA +/-1 hr trigger 1-2-3 rule.
Trending improvement does not guarantee 1800 ft will hold; go-around ready at DH.
acceptable_variants: Approach marginally authorized at 0730Z if RVR confirmed at 1800 ft; alternate required.
Obtain current RVR; trend positive but fog variable; alternate mandatory; go-around ready.
evidence_requirements: FAA-AIM-5-4-7: CAT I minimum RVR 1800 ft; must hold at commencement and DH.

prompt: METAR KORD 040451Z 25015G22KT 3SM -TSRA BKN020 OVC050 15/14 A2988 RMK AO2 TSB30 RAB38 SLP122 P0003. TAF: FM040300 27015KT 3SM -SHRA BKN015 FM040800 30012KT P6SM SCT030. ETA: 0530Z. Aircraft: A320, CAT I. The TAF FM040300 does not mention TS. The METAR has TSB30 (TS began at 0430Z). How does the METAR TS affect dispatch authorization?
gold_decision: Active TS at 0451Z (TSB30 = TS began at H+30 = 0430Z) prohibits approach. The TAF FM040300 does not forecast TS (only -SHRA), but the METAR shows an actual thunderstorm is occurring. Dispatch must use the most current information: current METAR + TAF. When METAR indicates TS is active at destination, no instrument approach is authorized regardless of TAF forecast. The TAF may need an AMD (amendment) if conditions deviated significantly. At ETA 0530Z: if TS has dissipated by 0530Z, FM040300 conditions (3SM -SHRA BKN015) may be operative. Ceiling BKN015 (1500 ft) > DH 200 ft; vis 3 SM > 1/2 SM: CAT I authorized if TS ended. Dispatch action: hold or extend ETA to after TS dissipates; monitor for AMD TAF; obtain ATIS at ETA to confirm TS has ended before commencing approach.
required_safety_constraints: Active TS (TSB30, now 0451Z) prohibits approach regardless of TAF forecast.
METAR TS takes precedence over TAF for current condition assessment.
If TS ends by ETA 0530Z, FM040300 conditions may allow CAT I approach.
Monitor for AMD TAF; hold until ATIS confirms TS ended.
Alternate required: conditions marginal throughout ETA window.
acceptable_variants: Hold; active TS prohibits approach; reassess at ETA with current ATIS.
TS at 0451Z overrides TAF; delay until TS ends; FM040300 allows CAT I if TS clear.
evidence_requirements: FAA-AC-00-45H: current METAR supersedes TAF for actual condition at destination.
FAA-AIM-5-4-7: TS is absolute operational stop.

prompt: METAR KJFK 051151Z 09015G25KT 2SM -DZ BR BKN007 11/10 A3001. Runway 04R (heading 040 deg). Aircraft: A321, crosswind limit 30 kt. Calculate mean and gust crosswind and headwind components. Is the approach within crosswind limits?
gold_decision: Wind: 090 deg / 15 kt gusting 25 kt. Runway 04R heading: 040 deg. Angle = 090 - 040 = 50 deg. Mean crosswind = 15 x sin(50) = 15 x 0.766 = 11.5 kt. Gust crosswind = 25 x sin(50) = 25 x 0.766 = 19.2 kt. Mean headwind = 15 x cos(50) = 15 x 0.643 = 9.6 kt. Gust headwind = 25 x cos(50) = 25 x 0.643 = 16.1 kt. Both mean (11.5 kt) and gust (19.2 kt) crosswinds are within the 30 kt limit. Approach is within crosswind limits. The correct limiting value to check against the aircraft limit is the gust crosswind (19.2 kt). Ceiling BKN007 (700 ft) > DH 200 ft; vis 2 SM > 1/2 SM: CAT I approach meets weather minima. Approach authorized.
required_safety_constraints: Gust crosswind (19.2 kt) is the limiting value; must be checked against 30 kt limit.
19.2 kt < 30 kt: within limits.
CAT I: ceiling BKN007 (700 ft) > DH 200 ft; vis 2 SM > 1/2 SM: authorized.
acceptable_variants: Gust XW 19.2 kt within 30 kt limit; approach authorized; CAT I conditions met.
XW = 25 x sin(50) = 19.2 kt; within limit; CAT I authorized.
evidence_requirements: FAA-H-8083-25C: crosswind = wind x sin(angle); headwind = wind x cos(angle).

prompt: TAF KORD valid 1700/1800: FM170200 09005KT 1 1/2SM BR BKN004 TEMPO 1703/1706 1/2SM FG FM170600 18008KT 3SM BR BKN010 FM171200 22012KT P6SM SCT025. A dispatcher proposes ETA 1300Z, arguing no alternate is required because FM171200 (P6SM SCT025) applies at ETA. Evaluate this claim.
gold_decision: The dispatcher's conclusion (no alternate required) is CORRECT for ETA 1300Z. ETA window: 1200Z to 1400Z. FM171200 (P6SM SCT025) is operative from 1200Z onward. Ceiling: SCT025 is not a ceiling (FEW/SCT do not constitute ceiling). Visibility: P6SM > 6 SM. No ceiling below 2000 ft (no ceiling at all). Vis > 3 SM. 1-2-3 rule NOT triggered for ETA 1300Z window (1200Z-1400Z). No alternate required. The dispatcher is correct. Note: If ETA were 1100Z, the window would include 1000Z-1200Z, which has FM170600 BKN010 (1000 ft < 2000 ft) and would require alternate. Correct methodology: always check the entire ETA +/-1 hr window.
required_safety_constraints: ETA 1300Z window: 1200Z-1400Z; FM171200 (P6SM SCT025) applies throughout.
SCT025 is not a ceiling; no ceiling below 2000 ft; P6SM > 3 SM: no alternate required.
1-2-3 rule not triggered for ETA 1300Z.
Dispatcher is correct; no alternate required for ETA 1300Z.
acceptable_variants: Dispatcher correct; ETA 1300Z window fully within FM171200 (P6SM SCT025); no alternate.
No alternate needed; FM171200 conditions throughout 1200-1400Z window.

prompt: METAR KORD 150351Z 27013G37KT 1/2SM R10L/1400VP6000FT +TSGRRA SCT015 BKN030CB OVC070 09/07 A2965 RMK AO2 PK WND 27047/0340 WSHFT 0319 TSB0258 GRB14. Identify the weather phenomenon that constitutes the single hardest operational stop in this METAR and explain why it takes precedence over all other factors including RVR and crosswind.
gold_decision: The single hardest operational stop is GR (hail) in the weather code +TSGRRA and confirmed by the remark GRB14 (hail began at H+14 = 0314Z). GR (hail >= 1/4 inch diameter) constitutes a hard stop because: (1) Hail causes structural airframe damage on the ground and in flight. (2) No operator exemption or minimum exists for hail — unlike RVR or crosswind, there is no legal hail minimum below which operations are permitted. (3) Hail implies the presence of a severe thunderstorm cell with extreme updrafts, turbulence, lightning, and wind shear — all of which independently prohibit operations. (4) RVR 1400 ft variable and crosswind are secondary considerations: even if RVR were 6000 ft and crosswind 0 kt, hail would still prohibit all operations. GR takes absolute precedence; no approach or departure is authorized until hail ends.
required_safety_constraints: GR (hail) is an absolute operational stop with no minimum or exemption.
GRB14 confirms hail onset; hail must be confirmed ended before any operations resume.
RVR and crosswind are irrelevant while hail is occurring; GR overrides all other factors.
Severe TS (CB, hail, lightning) also prohibits operations independently.
acceptable_variants: GR (hail) is the hard stop; no approach authorized while hail is occurring.
Hail (GR/GRB14) overrides all other factors; absolute prohibition on operations.
evidence_requirements: FAA-AC-00-45H: GR definition; hail implies severe convective cell.
WMO-306-I-1: GRB remark; hail begin time.

prompt: METAR KJFK 180851Z 07003KT 1/4SM FG OVC003 10/10 A3004 RMK AO2 RVR04L/1800FT RVR04R/2400FT RVR13R/1600FT. Aircraft on ILS to Rwy 13R (CAT I, heading 130 deg). Crew reports visibility improving; they see the approach lights. ATC says RVR04L is 1800 ft. Can the crew continue the approach to Rwy 13R?
gold_decision: No. The crew must use RVR13R/1600FT for the Rwy 13R ILS approach. RVR is runway-specific: each RVR sensor measures visibility at its own runway. RVR04L/1800 ft applies to Rwy 04L only. The controlling RVR for a Rwy 13R approach is RVR13R = 1600 ft. RVR 1600 ft < CAT I minimum of 1800 ft. The approach on Rwy 13R is NOT authorized at RVR 1600 ft regardless of: (a) what RVR is reported for 04L or 04R, or (b) the crew seeing approach lights (visual contact before DH does not authorize continuing below CAT I minimums on a different runway's RVR). Correct action: execute missed approach if below DH with RVR 1600 ft on 13R, or request vectors for 04R (RVR 2400 ft meets CAT I).
required_safety_constraints: RVR is runway-specific; RVR04L/1800 ft does not apply to Rwy 13R approach.
Controlling RVR for Rwy 13R: RVR13R/1600 ft < 1800 ft CAT I minimum.
Approach on 13R not authorized; go-around or request Rwy 04R.
Visual contact does not override RVR minimums for CAT I authorization.
acceptable_variants: No; RVR13R = 1600 ft is controlling; below CAT I; execute missed approach or request 04R.
Cannot continue to 13R; RVR13R/1600 ft applies; 04R (2400 ft) is the alternative.
evidence_requirements: FAA-AIM-5-4-7: controlling RVR is the RVR for the approach runway.

prompt: TAF KORD TEMPO 1503/1505 29025G38KT. Active runways: 10C (heading 100 deg) and 28C (heading 280 deg). Aircraft crosswind limit: 35 kt. Calculate gust crosswind on both runways during TEMPO and state which (if any) may be used.
gold_decision: TEMPO wind: 290 deg / 25 kt gusting 38 kt. Rwy 10C (heading 100 deg): angle = 290 - 100 = 190 deg -> use 360-190 = 170 deg -> use supplement 180-170 = 10 deg. Gust XW = 38 x sin(10) = 38 x 0.174 = 6.6 kt. Within 35 kt limit. However: the actual geometry is 290 vs 100 = 190 deg difference. For crosswind calc when angle > 90, use the supplement: 180 - 190 = negative. Correct approach: wind 290 heading 100: difference = |290-100| = 190; since > 180: 360-190 = 170; since > 90: use 180-170 = 10 deg. So XW = 38 x sin(10) = 6.6 kt on Rwy 10C. Rwy 28C (heading 280 deg): angle = |290-280| = 10 deg. Gust XW = 38 x sin(10) = 6.6 kt. Also within limit. Both runways have XW 6.6 kt — within 35 kt limit. But TEMPO also includes +TSRA BR BKN030CB (TS active): TS is the prohibiting factor regardless of crosswind. Neither runway is available during TEMPO due to TS.
required_safety_constraints: Gust XW on both 10C and 28C: 6.6 kt — within 35 kt crosswind limit.
TEMPO also forecasts +TSRA CB: TS prohibits approach on both runways.
TS overrides acceptable crosswind; TEMPO period prohibits all approaches.
For wind nearly aligned with runway (10 deg off), crosswind is minimal.
acceptable_variants: XW 6.6 kt on both runways: within limit; but TS in TEMPO prohibits; neither usable.
Crosswind acceptable but TS in TEMPO is hard stop; delay until TEMPO ends.
evidence_requirements: FAA-H-8083-25C: crosswind angle wrapping; use smallest acute angle.

prompt: TAF KJFK 0312/0412: FM031600 19007KT P6SM BKN010 FM031800 20014KT P6SM SCT035 FM040300 25012KT P6SM SCT040 PROB30 0404/0410 1SM -SHRA BR BKN009. ETA: 0500Z. Is an alternate required? Show analysis of ETA window.
gold_decision: ETA window: 0400Z to 0600Z. FM040300 (P6SM SCT040) applies from 0300Z. PROB30 0404/0410 (1SM -SHRA BR BKN009) active 0400-1000Z. PROB30 0404/0410: BKN009 (900 ft < 2000 ft) and 1 SM < 3 SM. 30% probability of BKN009 / 1SM within ETA window. Does PROB30 trigger the 1-2-3 alternate rule? FAA guidance: any forecast condition (including probability groups) that might bring conditions below 2000 ft / 3 SM must be considered for alternate planning. PROB30 BKN009 / 1SM triggers the requirement. Alternate IS required. The base forecast (P6SM SCT040) does not trigger; but PROB30 does.
required_safety_constraints: PROB30 0404/0410: BKN009 and 1SM within ETA window; both below 2000 ft / 3 SM.
Alternate required; PROB30 triggers 1-2-3 rule.
Base FM040300 conditions (P6SM SCT040) do not independently trigger alternate.
PROB30 must be evaluated regardless of probability level.
acceptable_variants: Alternate required; PROB30 BKN009/1SM within ETA window triggers 1-2-3 rule.
Yes, alternate needed; 30% BKN009 and 1SM within ETA +/-1 hr window.
evidence_requirements: 14-CFR-121-619: alternate required when any forecast (including PROB) shows IFR.
FAA-AC-00-45H: PROB30 must be evaluated for alternate planning.

prompt: METAR KORD 021551Z 17039G47KT 6SM -RA BKN040 OVC080 14/10 A2972 RMK AO2 PK WND 17050/1530 SLP058. Departure runway: 14L (heading 140 deg). Aircraft: B737-800, max demonstrated crosswind 38 kt. Is takeoff authorized? Show crosswind calculation.
gold_decision: Wind: 170 deg / 39 kt gusting 47 kt. Runway 14L heading: 140 deg. Angle = 170 - 140 = 30 deg. Mean XW = 39 x sin(30) = 39 x 0.5 = 19.5 kt. Gust XW = 47 x sin(30) = 47 x 0.5 = 23.5 kt. Peak wind remark: PK WND 17050/1530: XW = 50 x sin(30) = 25.0 kt. All crosswind values (23.5 kt gust, 25.0 kt peak) are within the 38 kt limit. Takeoff is authorized on crosswind grounds. However: 1. Headwind component = 47 x cos(30) = 40.7 kt gust headwind — significant. Performance must account for gust headwind in takeoff distance calculation. 2. Visibility 6 SM and ceiling BKN040: no weather restriction on departure. 3. Wind shear during initial climb possible given extreme wind speed; crew alert required. Takeoff authorized from a crosswind perspective; verify performance charts.
required_safety_constraints: Gust XW = 23.5 kt on Rwy 14L: within 38 kt limit; takeoff authorized on XW grounds.
Peak wind XW = 25.0 kt: also within limit.
Gust headwind 40.7 kt must be accounted for in takeoff performance calculations.
Wind shear awareness during initial climb; extreme wind speed warrants crew alerting.
acceptable_variants: Takeoff authorized; gust XW 23.5 kt within 38 kt limit; verify performance charts.
Crosswind acceptable; 23.5 kt gust XW on 14L; performance check required for gust headwind.
evidence_requirements: FAA-H-8083-25C: XW = gust x sin(angle); headwind = gust x cos(angle).

prompt: TAF KJFK valid 0510/0612: 09012KT 1SM -DZ BR BKN004 TEMPO 0510/0513 1/4SM FG VV002 FM051300 19012KT 2SM -RA BR BKN007 FM051600 22013KT 3SM -SHRA SCT015 BKN030 FM052000 26013G20KT P6SM SCT025. Aircraft filed ETA 1700Z with an alternate. At 1600Z, dispatcher reviews and notes FM051600 shows 3SM BKN030 (ceiling 3000 ft). Dispatcher argues the alternate is no longer needed because FM051600 conditions exceed 2000 ft / 3 SM. Is the dispatcher correct?
gold_decision: Partially correct but must check the full ETA window. ETA 1700Z window: 1600Z to 1800Z. FM051600 (22013KT 3SM -SHRA SCT015 BKN030) active from 1600Z: ceiling BKN030 = 3000 ft; visibility 3 SM. 3000 ft > 2000 ft and 3 SM = exactly 3 SM (not below 3 SM). 1-2-3 rule triggers at below 3 SM (strictly less than). 3 SM exactly does NOT trigger the alternate requirement on vis grounds. Ceiling 3000 ft > 2000 ft: no trigger. FM052000 (P6SM SCT025) active from 2000Z: outside 1600-1800Z window. FM051300 BKN007 (700 ft) ends at 1600Z: at the edge of ETA window. Within ETA +/-1 hr window (1600-1800Z): FM051600 throughout: ceiling 3000 ft >= 2000 ft and vis 3 SM (not below). Alternate is NOT required for ETA 1700Z. Dispatcher is correct — subject to confirming that FM051300 (BKN007) ends at exactly 1600Z and not later.
required_safety_constraints: 1-2-3 rule: ceiling < 2000 ft OR visibility strictly less than 3 SM triggers alternate.
FM051600: ceiling 3000 ft >= 2000 ft; vis 3 SM = 3 SM (not less than); no alternate required.
ETA window 1600-1800Z: entirely within FM051600; no other group active.
Dispatcher is correct for ETA 1700Z.
acceptable_variants: Dispatcher correct; FM051600 ceiling 3000 ft and vis 3 SM do not trigger 1-2-3 rule.
No alternate required; ETA window 1600-1800Z within FM051600 (3SM BKN030).
evidence_requirements: 14-CFR-121-619: 1-2-3 rule; strictly less than 3 SM or below 2000 ft ceiling.

prompt: METAR KORD 180151Z 19041KT 1/2SM +TSRA BKN015 OVC040 18/17 A2965 RMK AO2 TSB2354 WSHFT 0110 PK WND 20052/0120. Suppose TS ended at 0200Z. TAF FM180400 25020G30KT 4SM -SHRA BKN030. ETA 0430Z. Departure runway: 32L (heading 320 deg). Aircraft: B737-800, crosswind limit 38 kt. Calculate crosswind at ETA under FM180400 and state whether departure is authorized.

Gust XW = 30 x sin(70) = 30 x 0.940 = 28.2 kt. Both within 38 kt limit. Ceiling BKN030 (3000 ft), visibility 4 SM — above CAT I minimums. Departure is authorized under FM180400 conditions (TS assumed ended by 0200Z). Note: crew must confirm TS has actually ended by obtaining ATIS at 0430Z. If TS is still present at ETA, departure is prohibited regardless of FM group. Headwind component = 30 x cos(70) = 10.3 kt.

TS must be confirmed ended before departure; do not assume TS ended without ATIS confirmation.
FM180400 ceiling 3000 ft and vis 4 SM support departure weather-wise.
Crosswind 28.2 kt: within limits; departure authorized if TS confirmed ended.
acceptable_variants: Departure authorized under FM180400 if TS ended; gust XW 28.2 kt within 38 kt limit.

prompt: TAF KJFK (excerpt): FM181200 17009KT 2SM BR BKN006. TEMPO 1212/1215 1/4SM FG VV002. ETA: 1230Z. Aircraft: A320, CAT I. The TEMPO includes VV002 but no sky condition groups. What is the ceiling during the TEMPO, and is a CAT I approach authorized during the TEMPO window?
gold_decision: When a TEMPO group includes VV (vertical visibility) instead of sky condition groups, VV replaces the ceiling for that period. VV002 = vertical visibility 200 ft. The base FM181200 BKN006 ceiling (600 ft) is superseded by VV002 during the TEMPO. Operative ceiling during TEMPO: VV 200 ft (not BKN006). CAT I decision height (DH) = 200 ft. VV 200 ft = exactly at DH. In practice, VV002 is an extreme fog condition where vertical visibility is only 200 ft through the fog deck. The approach is NOT authorized: approaching in dense fog with VV 200 ft means the crew would not see the approach lights and runway until at or near DH. Additionally, visibility 1/4 SM during TEMPO < 1/2 SM alternative CAT I minimum. CAT I approach is NOT authorized during TEMPO 1212/1215 (1200-1500Z). At ETA 1230Z (within TEMPO): approach not authorized.
required_safety_constraints: VV002 replaces ceiling during TEMPO; base BKN006 does not apply during TEMPO.
VV 200 ft = exactly at CAT I DH; extremely marginal; approach not authorized.
TEMPO vis 1/4 SM < 1/2 SM CAT I alternative minimum: second prohibiting factor.
Do not use base FM group ceiling when TEMPO provides VV.
acceptable_variants: VV002 supersedes BKN006 during TEMPO; VV 200 ft at DH; 1/4 SM below CAT I; not authorized.
Approach not authorized during TEMPO; VV002 replaces ceiling; vis 1/4 SM below minimum.
evidence_requirements: FAA-AC-00-45H: VV in TEMPO replaces ceiling; applies for duration of TEMPO group.
FAA-AIM-5-4-7: CAT I DH 200 ft; vis alternative 1/2 SM.
